BMABMDR 0.1.20
  • Reference
  • Articles
    • Introduction to BMABMDR: Bayesian Model Averaging for Benchmark Dose Analysis
    • Litter Effects in BMABMDR
  • Changelog

Litter Effects in BMABMDR

Source: vignettes/Litter_Effect.Rmd
Litter_Effect.Rmd

Quantal Data

The key distinction is the data format:

  • Standard quantal (data_quantal): one row per dose group, columns = dose, response (total affected), size (total N)
  • Quantal with litter (data_quantal_litter): one row per litter, columns = dose, y (affected in that litter), n (litter size)
library(BMABMDR)

# --- Standard quantal (no litter): one row per dose group ---
data("data_quantal")
data_quantal
##   dose response size
## 1    0        0   20
## 2    5        4   20
## 3   15        6   20
## 4   50        5   20
## 5  100       12   20
#>   dose response size
#> 1    0        0   20
#> 2    5        4   20
#> 3   15        6   20
#> 4   50        5   20
#> 5  100       12   20

data_Q <- PREP_DATA_QA(data = data_quantal, sumstats = TRUE, q = 0.1)
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 1: Global Deviance = 14.6439 
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 2: Global Deviance = 14.6439
fit_q  <- full.laplaceQ_MA(data.Q = data_Q, prior.weights = rep(1, 8))
fit_q$MA
##       BMDL        BMD       BMDU 
##  0.5582023  2.7904145 14.4418567
# --- Quantal with litter: one row per litter ---
data("data_quantal_litter")
head(data_quantal_litter)
##   dose y  n
## 1    0 1 12
## 2    0 1 12
## 3    0 0 12
## 4    0 0 13
## 5    0 0  1
## 6    0 0 13
#>   dose y  n
#> 1    0 1 12
#> 2    0 1 12
#> 3    0 0 12
#> ...
# Each row = one litter: y = affected pups, n = litter size

data_QL <- PREP_DATA_QA(data = data_quantal_litter,
                         sumstats = TRUE,   # each row is a litter summary
                         q = 0.1,
                         cluster = TRUE)    # activates Beta-Binomial
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 1: Global Deviance = 328.0836 
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 2: Global Deviance = 326.2945 
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 3: Global Deviance = 326.1379 
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 4: Global Deviance = 326.126 
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 5: Global Deviance = 326.1251 
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 1: Global Deviance = 327.8699 
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 2: Global Deviance = 326.0807 
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 3: Global Deviance = 325.9259 
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 4: Global Deviance = 325.9142 
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 5: Global Deviance = 325.9134
fit_ql  <- full.laplaceQ_MA(data.Q = data_QL, prior.weights = rep(1, 8))
fit_ql$MA
##     BMDL      BMD     BMDU 
## 1.512957 4.106087 7.404410
# --- Converting individual binary data to litter-level format ---
# Suppose you have raw individual binary outcomes:
set.seed(123)
indiv_quantal <- data.frame(
  dose   = rep(c(0, 5, 15, 50, 100), each = 200),
  litter = rep(rep(1:10, each = 20), 5),  # 10 litters per dose, 20 pups each
  y      = c(rbinom(200, 1, 0.05),        # dose 0:   5% background
             rbinom(200, 1, 0.10),         # dose 5:  10%
             rbinom(200, 1, 0.20),         # dose 15: 20%
             rbinom(200, 1, 0.35),         # dose 50: 35%
             rbinom(200, 1, 0.60))         # dose 100: 60%
)
head(indiv_quantal)
##   dose litter y
## 1    0      1 0
## 2    0      1 0
## 3    0      1 0
## 4    0      1 0
## 5    0      1 0
## 6    0      1 0
#>   dose litter y
#> 1    0      1 0
#> 2    0      1 0
#> ...

# Aggregate to litter-level (one row per litter)
library(dplyr)
litter_quantal <- indiv_quantal |>
  group_by(dose, litter) |>
  summarise(
    y = sum(y),     # number affected in this litter
    n = n(),        # litter size
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::select(dose, y, n) |>  # drop litter ID — PREP_DATA_QA doesn't use it
  as.data.frame()

litter_quantal
##    dose  y  n
## 1     0  2 20
## 2     0  2 20
## 3     0  0 20
## 4     0  0 20
## 5     0  1 20
## 6     0  2 20
## 7     0  2 20
## 8     0  0 20
## 9     0  0 20
## 10    0  1 20
## 11    5  2 20
## 12    5  2 20
## 13    5  2 20
## 14    5  3 20
## 15    5  1 20
## 16    5  0 20
## 17    5  3 20
## 18    5  3 20
## 19    5  2 20
## 20    5  3 20
## 21   15  4 20
## 22   15  4 20
## 23   15  3 20
## 24   15  3 20
## 25   15  5 20
## 26   15  3 20
## 27   15  5 20
## 28   15  6 20
## 29   15  5 20
## 30   15  8 20
## 31   50  8 20
## 32   50 10 20
## 33   50  8 20
## 34   50  5 20
## 35   50  5 20
## 36   50  5 20
## 37   50 10 20
## 38   50  5 20
## 39   50  7 20
## 40   50  6 20
## 41  100 14 20
## 42  100 10 20
## 43  100 11 20
## 44  100 12 20
## 45  100 11 20
## 46  100 12 20
## 47  100 10 20
## 48  100 16 20
## 49  100 13 20
## 50  100 12 20
#>    dose  y  n
#> 1     0  1 20
#> 2     0  0 20
#> 3     0  2 20
#> 4     5  3 20
#> ...  (10 litters × 5 doses = 50 rows)

# Now fit with litter effect
data_QL2 <- PREP_DATA_QA(data = litter_quantal,
                          sumstats = TRUE,
                          q = 0.1,
                          cluster = TRUE)
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 1: Global Deviance = 234.3694
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 2: Global Deviance = 178.6618
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 3: Global Deviance = 181.2098
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 4: Global Deviance = 179.1887
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 5: Global Deviance = 178.5223
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 6: Global Deviance = 177.5629
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 7: Global Deviance = 176.6916
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 8: Global Deviance = 176.7675
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 9: Global Deviance = 178.2182
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 10: Global Deviance = 1314.83
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 11: Global Deviance = 175.3617
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 12: Global Deviance = 177.3923
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 13: Global Deviance = 176.663
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 14: Global Deviance = 177.8285
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 15: Global Deviance = 178.5953
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 16: Global Deviance = 175.6158
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 17: Global Deviance = 175.7247
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 18: Global Deviance = 175.6958
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 19: Global Deviance = 178.1266
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 20: Global Deviance = 175.787
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 1: Global Deviance = 234.0624
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 2: Global Deviance = 177.6873
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 3: Global Deviance = 176.5375
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 4: Global Deviance = 177.4381
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 5: Global Deviance = 183.6169
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 6: Global Deviance = 176.5846
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 7: Global Deviance = 183.8566
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 8: Global Deviance = 183.7616
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 9: Global Deviance = 183.666
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 10: Global Deviance = 179.9611
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 11: Global Deviance = 176.7587
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 12: Global Deviance = 188.7793
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 13: Global Deviance = 188.4816
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 14: Global Deviance = 187.693
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 15: Global Deviance = 181.4883
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 16: Global Deviance = 187.4898
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 17: Global Deviance = 189.2509
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 18: Global Deviance = 187.53
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 19: Global Deviance = 186.3777
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 20: Global Deviance = 177.3684
fit_ql2  <- full.laplaceQ_MA(data.Q = data_QL2, prior.weights = rep(1, 8))
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 1 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 2 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 2 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 2 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 1 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 3 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 3 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 1 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 1 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
## Error in chol.default(-H) : the leading minor of order 4 is not positive
fit_ql2$MA
##     BMDL      BMD     BMDU 
## 15.30839 22.84756 34.20808

The conversion pipeline: individual binary → group_by(dose, litter) → summarise(y = sum(y), n = n()) → pass to PREP_DATA_QA(..., cluster = TRUE).

The cluster = TRUE flag switches the likelihood from Binomial to Beta-Binomial, adding the ICC parameter ρ\rho that accounts for within-litter correlation.

Continuous Data

For continuous endpoints measured on individual animals within litters (or cages), BMABMDR uses a multivariate Normal likelihood with compound-symmetry correlation structure. An extra parameter ρ\rho (intra-litter correlation) captures the within-cluster dependence.

Key differences from the standard (non-clustered) continuous workflow:

Aspect Standard Clustered
Data level Summary (Mean/SD/N per dose) Individual (one row per animal)
Columns required dose, response, size dose, response, litter
Prep function PREP_DATA_N() / PREP_DATA_LN() PREP_DATA_N_C() / PREP_DATA_LN_C()
Fit function full.laplace_MA() full.laplace_MAc()
Models 16 (8 shapes × {Normal, Lognormal}) 16 (same shapes, same error families)
Extra parameter — ρ\rho (ICC, PERT prior on [0,1][0,1])

Real-data example: Egg-Shell Thickness

The package ships EggShellThickness.csv, a study where hens were exposed to different concentrations and eggshell thickness was measured per cage.

library(BMABMDR)

# EggShellThickness.csv lives in data/ in the source tree (not inst/extdata),
# so it is not installed with the package. We locate it relative to the
# vignette source directory.
eggshell_path <- here::here("data", "EggShellThickness.csv")
if (!file.exists(eggshell_path)) {
  # Fallback when rendering from vignettes/

  eggshell_path <- file.path("..", "data", "EggShellThickness.csv")
}
eggshell <- read.csv(eggshell_path)

head(eggshell)
##   Exposure Cage Eggshell.Thickness
## 1        0    1              0.191
## 2        0    2              0.200
## 3        0    3              0.190
## 4        0    4              0.197
## 5        0    5              0.203
## 6        0    6              0.169
#>   Exposure Cage Eggshell.Thickness
#> 1        0    1              0.191
#> 2        0    2              0.200

# Columns must be in order: dose, response, litter (cluster)
egg_input <- data.frame(
  dose     = eggshell$Exposure,
  response = eggshell[["Eggshell.Thickness"]],
  litter   = eggshell$Cage
)

# Prepare data for Normal and Lognormal clustered models
data_N  <- PREP_DATA_N_C(egg_input, q = 0.05, prior.d = "N11")
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 1: Global Deviance = -87.9376 
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 2: Global Deviance = -91.7366 
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 3: Global Deviance = -118.8249
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 1: Global Deviance = -409.5696 
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 2: Global Deviance = -409.5696
data_LN <- PREP_DATA_LN_C(egg_input, q = 0.05, prior.d = "N11")
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 1: Global Deviance = -87.9376 
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 2: Global Deviance = -91.7366 
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 3: Global Deviance = -118.8249
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 1: Global Deviance = -409.5696 
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 2: Global Deviance = -409.5696
# Fit model-averaged BMD (Laplace)
fit_c <- full.laplace_MAc(data.N = data_N, data.LN = data_LN,
                           prior.weights = rep(1, 16))
## [1] "Best fitting model fits sufficiently well (Bayes factor is 2.69e+00)."
fit_c$MA
##     BMDL      BMD     BMDU 
## 231.4816 303.7270 375.6134

Foetal-weight example (das5)

The built-in das5 dataset contains individual foetal weights nested within litters. This is the example used in ?PREP_DATA_N_C:

data(das5)
data_input <- data.frame(
  dose     = das5$data$dose,
  response = das5$data$foetalweight,
  litter   = das5$data$number
)
head(data_input)
##   dose response litter
## 1    0     4.11      2
## 2    0     4.22      2
## 3    0     4.27      2
## 4    0     4.26      2
## 5    0     4.03      2
## 6    0     4.13      2
data_N2  <- PREP_DATA_N_C(data_input, q = 0.05, prior.d = "N11")
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 1: Global Deviance = -29.0541 
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 2: Global Deviance = -29.0541
## Warning in PREP_DATA_N_C(data_input, q = 0.05, prior.d = "N11"): the data do not contain information on the asymptote, and the default prior for fold change has been based on half the observed maximum;
##             please provide prior input on the maximum response by specifying 'maxy', if available
## default prior choices used on background
## default prior choices used on fold change
## default prior choices used on BMD
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 1: Global Deviance = 1984.496 
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 2: Global Deviance = 1984.496
## Warning in fp(xx): The origing of the x variable has been shifted by 1e-14
## Warning in fp(xx): The origing of the x variable has been shifted by 1e-14
## Warning in PREP_DATA_N_C(data_input, q = 0.05, prior.d = "N11"): distributional
## assumption of constant variance for the normal distribution is not met,
## Bartlett test p-value is 0.0009
## Warning in PREP_DATA_N_C(data_input, q = 0.05, prior.d = "N11"): distributional
## assumption of normality of residuals for the normal distribution is not met,
## Shapiro test p-value is 0.0000
data_LN2 <- PREP_DATA_LN_C(data_input, q = 0.05, prior.d = "N11")
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 1: Global Deviance = -29.0541 
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 2: Global Deviance = -29.0541
## Warning in PREP_DATA_LN_C(data_input, q = 0.05, prior.d = "N11"): the data do not contain information on the asymptote, and the default prior for fold change has been based on half the observed maximum;
##             please provide prior input on the maximum response by specifying 'maxy', if available
## default prior choices used on background
## default prior choices used on fold change
## default prior choices used on BMD
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 1: Global Deviance = 1984.496 
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 2: Global Deviance = 1984.496
## Warning in fp(xx): The origing of the x variable has been shifted by 1e-14
## Warning in fp(xx): The origing of the x variable has been shifted by 1e-14
## Warning in PREP_DATA_LN_C(data_input, q = 0.05, prior.d = "N11"):
## distributional assumption of constant variance for the lognormal distribution
## is not met, Bartlett test p-value is 0.0009
## Warning in PREP_DATA_LN_C(data_input, q = 0.05, prior.d = "N11"):
## distributional assumption of normality of residuals for the lognormal
## distribution is not met, Shapiro test p-value is 0.0000
fit_c2 <- full.laplace_MAc(data.N = data_N2, data.LN = data_LN2,
                             prior.weights = rep(1, 16))
## Warning in print(warning(bfTest$warn.bf)): Best fitting model fits sufficiently
## well (Bayes factor is 1.72e-06).
## [1] "Best fitting model fits sufficiently well (Bayes factor is 1.72e-06)."
fit_c2$MA
##     BMDL      BMD     BMDU 
## 428.3171 506.1398 591.3236

Simulated example: individual data with litter structure

If your data does not already have a cluster identifier you can simulate the workflow end-to-end:

set.seed(42)
n_litters  <- 5          # litters per dose group
litter_sz  <- 10         # pups per litter
doses      <- c(0, 10, 50, 100, 200)

sim_cont <- do.call(rbind, lapply(seq_along(doses), function(i) {
  d <- doses[i]
  do.call(rbind, lapply(1:n_litters, function(j) {
    # Litter-level random intercept
    litter_effect <- rnorm(1, mean = 0, sd = 0.3)
    # True dose-response: background 10, decreasing
    mu <- 10 - 3 * (d / max(doses)) + litter_effect
    data.frame(
      dose     = d,
      response = rnorm(litter_sz, mean = mu, sd = 0.5),
      litter   = paste0("D", d, "_L", j)
    )
  }))
}))

head(sim_cont)
##   dose response litter
## 1    0 10.12894  D0_L1
## 2    0 10.59285  D0_L1
## 3    0 10.72772  D0_L1
## 4    0 10.61342  D0_L1
## 5    0 10.35823  D0_L1
## 6    0 11.16705  D0_L1
# Prepare & fit
data_N3  <- PREP_DATA_N_C(sim_cont, q = 0.05, prior.d = "N11")
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 1: Global Deviance = -7.214 
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 2: Global Deviance = -7.214
## Warning in PREP_DATA_N_C(sim_cont, q = 0.05, prior.d = "N11"): the data do not contain information on the asymptote, and the default prior for fold change has been based on half the observed maximum;
##             please provide prior input on the maximum response by specifying 'maxy', if available
## default prior choices used on background
## default prior choices used on fold change
## default prior choices used on BMD
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 1: Global Deviance = 408.9552 
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 2: Global Deviance = 408.9552
## Warning in fp(xx): The origing of the x variable has been shifted by 1e-14
## Warning in fp(xx): The origing of the x variable has been shifted by 1e-14
## distributional assumption of constant variance for the normal distribution is met, Bartlett test p-value is 0.2329
## distributional assumption of normality of residuals for the normal distribution is met, Shapiro test p-value is 0.3214
data_LN3 <- PREP_DATA_LN_C(sim_cont, q = 0.05, prior.d = "N11")
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 1: Global Deviance = -7.214 
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 2: Global Deviance = -7.214
## Warning in PREP_DATA_LN_C(sim_cont, q = 0.05, prior.d = "N11"): the data do not contain information on the asymptote, and the default prior for fold change has been based on half the observed maximum;
##             please provide prior input on the maximum response by specifying 'maxy', if available
## default prior choices used on background
## default prior choices used on fold change
## default prior choices used on BMD
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 1: Global Deviance = 408.9552 
## GAMLSS-RS iteration 2: Global Deviance = 408.9552
## Warning in fp(xx): The origing of the x variable has been shifted by 1e-14
## Warning in fp(xx): The origing of the x variable has been shifted by 1e-14
## distributional assumption of constant variance for the lognormal distribution is met, Bartlett test p-value is 0.2329
## distributional assumption of normality of residuals for the lognormal distribution is met, Shapiro test p-value is 0.3109
fit_c3 <- full.laplace_MAc(data.N = data_N3, data.LN = data_LN3,
                             prior.weights = rep(1, 16))
## Warning in full.laplace_MAc(data.N = data_N3, data.LN = data_LN3, prior.weights
## = rep(1, : difficulties fitting the Probit (Lognormal) model; prior weight was
## set to 0 and the model is not included in model averaging
## Warning in (max.ll - lls[!is.na(lls)]) < 709 & prior.weights > 0: longer object
## length is not a multiple of shorter object length
## Warning in (max.ll - lls[!is.na(lls)]) < 709 & prior.weights > 0: longer object
## length is not a multiple of shorter object length
## Warning in print(warning(bfTest$warn.bf)): Best fitting model fits sufficiently
## well (Bayes factor is 9.47e-01).
## [1] "Best fitting model fits sufficiently well (Bayes factor is 9.47e-01)."
fit_c3$MA
##     BMDL      BMD     BMDU 
## 20.14073 29.70372 41.74148

Key points:

  • Data must be individual-level (one row per animal), with column order dose, response, litter.
  • PREP_DATA_N_C() / PREP_DATA_LN_C() replace the standard PREP_DATA_N() / PREP_DATA_LN().
  • full.laplace_MAc() replaces full.laplace_MA().
  • The Stan models internally estimate ρ\rho, the intra-litter correlation, using a compound-symmetry covariance structure.

Developed by C’ecile Kremer, Oluwafemi Olusoji, Marc Aerts, Ziv Shkedy, Jos’e Cortinas Abrahantes.

Site built with pkgdown 2.2.0.
